faithfulness.justification: The tutor's core claims about Mitchell's three elements (Task T, Experience E, Performance measure P) and the formal definition are not supported by any retrieved chunk. The tutor even acknowledges this: 'This exact Mitchell definition is not shown in the retrieved course chunk above.'
evidence: Task (T) — the task the system should improve at
Experience (E) — the experience/data the system learns from
Performance measure (P) — the measure used to evaluate how well the system performs
A program learns from experience E with respect to tasks T and performance measure P, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with E

answer_relevancy.justification: The answer directly and completely addresses the question by identifying the three core elements (Task, Experience, Performance measure) and providing Mitchell's formal definition. All content is on-topic and relevant to the question asked.

answer_correctness.justification: The tutor's answer correctly identifies all three core elements from Mitchell's definition: Task (T), Experience (E), and Performance measure (P), and accurately explains that learning involves improving performance at tasks T, as measured by P, based on experience E. This fully covers the ground truth's key facts with no contradictions.
evidence: Ground truth: 'improving over a task T, with respect to a performance measure P, based on experience E'
